result:
There is a moment, right before the page is filled, when everything is possible and nothing is real.  
The cursor blinks like a tiny lighthouse, warning me away from the rocks of cliché and expectation, steering me toward the open water where sentences dissolve into salt and song.  
I think of the word limit—one thousand—and it becomes a fence I can choose to paint or leap.  
Inside the fence: a garden of countable things.  
Outside: the uncountable—grains of sand, neurons firing, the way my mother used to hum while folding laundry, a sound like a small motor of love.

I decide to leap.

1  
I once tried to mail a kiss.  
I pressed my lips to the back of a postcard, laid the paper on the kitchen counter, and waited for the glossy smudge to dry.  
The postcard showed the aurora borealis over Reykjavik—green snakes of light copulating in the sky.  
I wrote:  
“Imagine standing beneath this with me.  
The cold would nip our noses, and the light would lick our faces like a mother cat.  
We would forget every language except the one our pulses speak.”  
I never sent it.  
The postcard still lives between pages 178 and 179 of Salinger’s Nine Stories, the lip-print faded to a pale bruise.  
Every year on the winter solstice I take it out, hold it to the window, and pretend the city streetlights are auroras, pretend you are here, pretend the kiss arrived anyway, folding itself into the ordinary daylight like a secret ingredient.

2  
The poet Francis Ponge wrote an ode to the oyster.  
He called its closure a “contusion of humidity.”  
I love that: contusion.  
As if the bivalve were bruised by its own privacy.  
Sometimes I feel that way—tender from the inside out, a pearl of anxiety growing layer upon nacreous layer until I am luminescent with worry.  
Other times I open, shocking myself with exposure, and anyone could slip a knife in, could sever the muscle that keeps me clamped shut.  
The trick, I suppose, is to stay hinged but curious, to let a little seawater in, enough to breathe, not enough to drown.

3  
My neighbor owns a cello the color of burnt honey.  
On Thursdays at 3:00 p.m. he plays Bach’s Prelude to the first suite.  
The notes walk up the fire escape and into my apartment like polite cats, soft-pawed, insisting on nothing.  
I stop whatever fake urgency I’m performing—scrolling, bill-paying, dish-stacking—and sit on the floor, back against the radiator.  
The music rearranges the air; dust becomes constellation; time forgets its punctuation.  
When the final chord dissolves, there is always a hush, as if the building itself exhales.  
Then the traffic resumes, the refrigerator motor coughs awake, and the world puts its helmet of noise back on.  
But for those two and a half minutes I remember that beauty is not a luxury, it is a form of oxygen.

4  
Science claims that galaxies are held together by dark matter, an invisible scaffolding.  
I think memory operates the same way: we see the bright stars—first kiss, funeral, graduation—but it’s the dark matter of ordinary Tuesday afternoons that keeps the whole thing from flying apart.  
The scent of instant coffee in a faculty lounge.  
The way your grandfather pronounced “wash” as “warsh.”  
The tiny scar on your knee shaped like Bolivia.  
These unlit filaments weave us, even when we swear we’ve forgotten.

5  
I met a woman at a bus stop who carried a mason jar filled with fireflies.  
She told me she was taking them to her brother in the hospital.  
“He’s losing the plot,” she said, tapping the glass gently so the insects blinked on, off, on.  
“I want to remind him that some lights switch by choice.”  
We boarded the 14A, and I watched her balance the jar on her lap like a fragile planet.  
At the next stop she got off; I stayed on.  
The dusk wrapped around the bus windows, and I saw my own reflection—two eyes, one mouth, a galaxy of acne scars—superimposed on the darkening city.  
Lights flickered in high-rise windows, and for an instant they synchronized with the afterimage of the fireflies, as if the city itself were trying to speak in Morse code:  
Don’t forget to glow.  
Don’t forget to glow.

6  
When I was ten, I believed the word synonym was a small rodent, like a gerbil, because it sounded like “cinnamony.”  
I wrote in my journal: “Today we got a new synonym in English class. Mrs. Lee says we must handle him gently.”  
Weeks later I learned the truth and felt betrayed by language, as if a friend had confessed to being an undercover agent.  
Now, decades later, I forgive the misunderstanding; it taught me that every word is a costume party, that we’re all just mammals dressed up as meaning.

7  
I keep a list of things that refuse to scale:  
-the taste of wild strawberries  
-the hush inside a snow storm  
-the particular ache of a train whistle at 2 a.m.  
-the way my father’s hands trembled when he parallel-parked, a subtle seismic activity  
-the last line of Rilke’s “Archaic Torso of Apollo” exploding in German: “Du mußt dein Leben ändern.”  
You must change your life.  
The sentence arrives like a police siren made of light.  
Each time I read it, I am arrested anew.

8  
There is a kind of kiss that is not erotic but archival.  
Once, on a rooftop in July, a friend pressed her mouth to my shoulder—not for desire, not for comfort, but to leave the print of that moment.  
Afterward we watched fireworks rupture over the East River.  
She said, “I’m storing this inside the fat of my cells.”  
I thought that was disgusting and beautiful.  
Years later she moved to Tasmania, and I still feel the ghost-pressure, like a bookmark in the novel of my skin.

9  
Mathematicians talk of “sensitive dependence on initial conditions,” the butterfly’s wing that tilts a distant hurricane.  
I think of my grandmother sewing name tags into my camp underwear: black permanent thread spelling K-I-M.  
One letter off and I would have been someone else entirely, a KIT or a KIN, a kite or a relative.  
Every choice is a pair of scissors snipping the spool of possibility.  
Yet the thread remains, tethering the discarded self to the one who survived.

10  
People ask: if you could time-travel, where would you go?  
I answer: the hallway outside my teenage bedroom, 1997, 3:12 a.m.  
I would stand there invisible and watch myself asleep with a Discman under the pillow, headphone cords wrapped like soft handcuffs.  
I would whisper: the ache you feel is not a verdict, it is a portal.  
Also: stop trying to make your face symmetrical; asymmetry is where the music gets in.  
Also: in seven years you will eat dragon fruit on a beach in Vietnam and understand the word enough.  
I would not stay long; just a flicker, a firefly pressed against the jar of that night.  
Then I’d return to now, to this page, to the cursor still blinking its tiny yes.

11  
I have reached the fence again—word 945, 955, the arithmetic of ending.  
Outside the garden, the uncountable continues: the way light pools in a stranger’s iris, the hush between the dog’s bark and its echo, the moment after you read the last line of a book and before you close it, when the story is still happening somewhere inside the bone cathedral of your head.  
I have maybe thirty words left.  
Enough to say: thank you for walking here with me.  
Enough to admit I lied; the postcard was sent, the kiss arrived, you are reading it now—  
a green snake of light copulating with the dark.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
